Tafseer:
The meaning of 'Adiyat':
'Adiyat' means 'those that run', but here it serves as an attribute of war horses. The proof is that the subsequent four attributes, which appear in sequential order, do not apply to anything other than war horses. Some have interpreted this as the camels of Muzdalifah, but there is no context for this; furthermore, the subsequent attributes cannot apply to camels.
Another opinion is that it refers to the horses of the warriors (ghazis), but there is no context for this restriction either. Most importantly, it would make the oaths completely disconnected from the subject of the oath (muqsam 'alayh).
The subject here is:
(Indeed, mankind, to his Lord, is ungrateful) (verse 6).
What connection can this subject have with the horses of the warriors!
This opinion, and the one regarding the camels of Muzdalifah mentioned above, both stem from the common delusion that the entity sworn by must be sacred. We repeatedly refute this—showing that being sacred is not a requirement for an oath; rather, the primary importance lies in its serving as a testimony to the claim coming after it. Further on, we will explain in detail how the characteristics of horses serve as a proof of human ungratefulness.
'Dabh' refers to the specific panting sound horses emit from their nostrils while running. This specific manner of panting is a clear testimony that they perform the task His power subserviated them for with complete loyalty and sacrifice, exhausting every ounce of their strength to fulfill their master's objective.

A style of the language: When a conjunction 'fa' (and) is used, as explained, it is a proof of sequential order, showing that all these attributes share the same subject.
'Muriyat' is derived from 'eera'', which means producing fire from flint or other objects.
'Qadh' here means striking, stamping, or knocking one thing against another.
The horses' energy and fiery feet: This describes their energy and speed in fulfilling their master's objective—showing that they run in a manner that sparks fly from their hooves. Since horses have iron shoes, when they run on rocky ground to attack the enemy, sparks fly like flint. It is as if they are running on hot coals to please their masters.

This states the primary objective for which they perform these sacrifices—namely, they launch sudden raids on the enemy. In Arabia, the most suitable time for raids on rivals was considered to be the morning; hence, the restriction 'in the morning' (subhan) is added. Their warning cry for raids, 'O morning!' (wa sabaha), also points to the morning in this aspect, to the extent that the word 'sabbaha' became a recognized term for launching a raid.

'Itharah' means raising or stirring up, and 'naq'' means dust.
If the particle 'bi' in 'bihi' is taken in the adverbial sense (dharf), referring to the morning ('subhan'), the meaning is: when they launch a raid in the morning, they stir up a cloud of dust. That is, their attack appears as if a storm wind has arrived from some direction.
And if 'bihi' is related to the running implied in 'adiyat', the meaning is: they stir up a cloud of dust through their running.
In both cases, the objective is to express their military significance—showing that their arrival is not like a morning breeze, but like a storm wind.

The particle 'bi' in 'bihi' is used here in the sense of accompaniment, and the pronoun refers to 'dust' (naq'an). That is: accompanied by this storm of dust, they penetrate directly into the center of the enemy's host, caring nothing for their spears and swords. They prioritize their masters' objective over their own lives, taking action without any fear, which is what fits them.

The primary claim: This is the core claim, to prove which the preceding oaths were sworn.
'Kanood' means ungrateful, selfish, and unappreciative of his master's favors.
The meaning is: the human who witnesses these sacrifices of horses and benefits from them, yet is not inspired to reflect that he too is the servant of his Lord and is obligated to remain obedient and committed like them, is highly ungrateful and mean. This is because they, as animals, recognize their master's right, while he, as a human, does not recognize his Lord's right.
Why horses are mentioned specifically: Note that horses are mentioned as an example, representing all the animals Allah has subserviated for man. The Qur'an has referred to them in various places to inspire gratitude. Particularly, the capabilities and services of the camel are mentioned repeatedly, inviting man to learn a lesson from its compliance, جفاکشی (hardiness), and patience: just as it obeys its master, man should obey the Lord Who subserviated such a massive and kothur-ul-manafe' (highly beneficial) animal to him.
Horses are mentioned specifically because they held great importance for war and defense in that era, when the responsibility of protecting the family and tribe fell on the family and tribe itself. Everyone had to maintain war horses to protect their families, and these horses were highly valued. They were the primary subject of Arab poetry. To estimate their taste, we present a verse of a warrior, who wrote:
'I spend my wealth on a strong and noble horse, whom I have made the guardian of my home, and then I have employed a servant to serve him.'
A specific lesson for man: This value and status of horses, obviously, is because of their services and sacrifices. If they did not perform these services, man would neither spend his wealth on them nor praise them. This proves that man is not unaware that a servant's value depends on his services, but he forgets this in his own case—desiring that he should not obey a single command of his Lord, yet receive the greatest reward in this world and the Hereafter.
There is another aspect to human ungratefulness: man is neither the creator of the horses nor of the resources upon which their sustenance depends, yet they serve him with dedication simply because God has subserviated them to him. In contrast, Allah is the Creator of man and of all his resources, yet he remains heedless of his duties to Him.

Man is a witness against himself: It is stated: there is no need to establish any external proof for his ungratefulness; he himself is a witness to it. This is identical to what was stated in Surah Al-Qiyamah:
(Rather, man, against himself, will be a witness, even if he presents his excuses) (Al-Qiyamah, 75:14-15).
The matters that are natural requirements of human nature do not depend on external proofs; their greatest testimony is present within man's own conscience. If he turns away from them, it is not because he lacks proof, but because they are contrary to his desires. Why else does he value only those horses that serve him, yet assume regarding his Lord that He will treat the righteous and the wicked as equal—treating him with favor in the Hereafter even if he does not obey His commands but lives as a slave to desires.

The testimony of character: This argues for his ungratefulness from his character—showing that he is infatuated with the love of wealth. He sees his horses sacrificing their lives and bringing everything they acquire to their master without demanding any rights, content with whatever is given; yet he, receiving everything from His favor, regards it as the fruit of his own capabilities and sits on it like a dragon, refusing to recognize His right, and if reminded, responds that his wealth has nothing to do with God:
(I was only given it because of knowledge I have) (Al-Qasas, 28:78).
'Khayr' here means wealth. This usage is common in classical Arabic and the Qur'an. The Being Who is the Creator and Owner of man, and from Whose favor he receives everything, is the One Who truly deserves love. The Qur'an has described the true believers: when their desires clash with His commands, they stand firm in their love for Him, rejecting their desires:
(But those who believe are stronger in love for Allah) (Al-Baqarah, 2:165).
In contrast, the ungrateful are worshippers of their wealth rather than their Lord.

Warning to the worshippers of wealth: This is a warning to the ungrateful and wealth-worshipping humans: do they not know that when what is in the graves is scattered, and what is in the breasts is obtained, everything will appear before them? While 'what is in the graves' refers to the dead, in the context of warning the wealthy, it also refers to the treasures which the misers dowerless bury in the earth. 'Bu'thira' means to scatter and lay open a collected thing for inspection—showing that nothing will remain hidden.

The motives of deeds will be recorded alongside the deeds: 'And obtained is what is in the breasts' (wa hussila ma fi-s-sindoor) - That is: like the treasures, all secrets of the breasts will be gathered so that the proof is established against everyone as to what deed they performed under which motive. Note that whenever a person commits an evil deed, he tries to fabricate a good motive to silence his own conscience and protect himself from criticism. Particularly, those who present themselves as religious or seek leadership must do so. They adopt such a cloak to hide their inner self. Such planners are warned in this verse: on that Day, the record of their motives will be laid before them alongside their deeds. Refer also to the final verses of the previous Surah—Az-Zalzalah.

This is stated to confirm the preceding warning. Allah Almighty is aware of everyone's deeds and motives even today, but displaying everything today is contrary to His wisdom. However, that Day is appointed specifically to lay open the entire record. When it is laid open, others will also realize how aware their Lord is of their secret and public deeds.
